coredata.dc:title: High biocompatible nitrogen and sulfur Co-doped carbon dots for Hg(II) detection and their long-term biological stability in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Waheed Ullah
Qin, Liying
Chen, Lixin
Khan, Wasim Ullah
Zeb, Shah
Khan, Asaf
Li, Shengzhen
Khan, Salim Ullah
Kamal, Sajid
Zhou, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots have been highly reported nanomaterials in recent times because of their excellent physio-chemical properties and various field of applications. Herein, a one-step hydrothermal approach was used to synthesize high biocompatible nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots, and examined their chemical sensing (Hg2+) and biological imaging properties. The N,S-CDs exhibited blue light, demonstrating a high quantum yield of up to 44.5% and excitation-independent fluorescent characteristics. Cytotoxicity was observed by CCK-8 assay using T-ca cells as a target source. Cell viability was recorded over 80% even after 7 days of treatment with a concentration up to 400 μg/mL, indicating low-toxicity of N,S-CDs. Notably, the bright blue fluorescence of N,S-CDs was quenched by introducing toxic Hg2+ ions into the solution. The detection limit was calculated to be about ∼3.5 nM, which is quite impressive compared to previous reports. Because of their low-toxicity, nano-size, and environment friendly properties, N,S-CDs could be excellent fluorescent agents for bio-imaging applications. The biological stability of fluorescent N,S-CDs was tested over time, and the findings were significant even after 8 days of incubation with T-ca cells. Because of good biocompatibility and bright fluorescence, N,S-CDs were suitable for in vivo imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Hg2+ detection
High biocompatible
Vitro/vivo-imaging
Biological stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of N,S-CDs/Hg2+ in actual samples
Long term bio-imaging performance in T-ca cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphically represent N,S-CDs and their applications as Hg2+ detection and vitro/vivo imaging.
TEM image with particle histogram and (b) High resolution TEM image of N,S-CDs.
UV–vis absorption, PLE spectrum under λ em 445 nm, and PL spectrum of the N,S-CDs monitoring under λ ex 360 nm.

(a) Response of the fluorescent N,S-CDs in the presence of various metal ions, F and F0 are the corresponding fluorescent intensities at 445 nm with and without metal ions, respectively. (b) Photographs of blank N,S-CDs (left) and with Hg2+ (right) under UV lamp. (c) Emission spectra of N,S-CDs with different concentrations from blank to 5 μM, λ ex = 360 nm. (d) Dependence of F/F0 on the concentration of Hg2+ within the range of 0–5 μM. Inset: dotted line shows the linear response of Hg2+ ranging from 0 to 100 nM.
(a) Absorption spectra of pure N,S-CDs and with Hg2+ ions. (b) PL lifetime decay carves of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs + Hg2+. (c) Observe morphology of N,S-CDs + Hg2+ complexes.
Kunming mice H&E-stained tissue slices (Heart, Liver, Spleen, Lung, Kidney) in PBS and N,S-CDs after 30 days.
Fluorescent image of T-ca cells incubated with pure N,S-CDs (a–d) 4 h. (e–h) 24 h. (i–l) 4 days. (m–p) 8 days. (All Scale bars are 50 μm).
Nude mice treated with N,S-CDs with subcutaneous injection (a) imaging after 30 min and (b) after 50 min.
Comparison of N,S-CDs with reported CDs for detection of Hg2+ ions.
Analytical performance of N,S-CDs to detect Hg2+ ions in actual samples (Tap water, River water and Milk).

head.ce:title: High biocompatible nitrogen and sulfur Co-doped carbon dots for Hg(II) detection and their long-term biological stability in living cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots have been highly reported nanomaterials in recent times because of their excellent physio-chemical properties and various field of applications. Herein, a one-step hydrothermal approach was used to synthesize high biocompatible nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots, and examined their chemical sensing (Hg2+) and biological imaging properties. The N,S-CDs exhibited blue light, demonstrating a high quantum yield of up to 44.5% and excitation-independent fluorescent characteristics. Cytotoxicity was observed by CCK-8 assay using T-ca cells as a target source. Cell viability was recorded over 80% even after 7 days of treatment with a concentration up to 400 μg/mL, indicating low-toxicity of N,S-CDs. Notably, the bright blue fluorescence of N,S-CDs was quenched by introducing toxic Hg2+ ions into the solution. The detection limit was calculated to be about ∼3.5 nM, which is quite impressive compared to previous reports. Because of their low-toxicity, nano-size, and environment friendly properties, N,S-CDs could be excellent fluorescent agents for bio-imaging applications. The biological stability of fluorescent N,S-CDs was tested over time, and the findings were significant even after 8 days of incubation with T-ca cells. Because of good biocompatibility and bright fluorescence, N,S-CDs were suitable for in vivo imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials have gained tremendous attention in recent decades because of their nanosize, excellent photoluminescent properties, tunable emission and multiple applications [1–7]. Recently, different fluorescent nanomaterials have been explored, including semiconductor quantum dots, optical nano-clusters, rare-earth emissive products, etc. Still, these materials contained toxic and heavy metals (Pb, Cs, Cd, Zn), low water solubility, and poor stability, hindering their broader application in sensing and medical fields [8–16]. Besides, carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging field in this era, which could be considered an alternative to fluorescent materials mentioned above. CDs have become very popular fluorescent nanomaterials due to high emission quantum yield (QY), excellent absorption and emission, high water solubility, low or non-toxicity, good bio-compatibility, and can be obtained by various and easy preparation methods [17–19]. Besides these outstanding physio-chemical properties, CDs have been applied in various fields such as chem-bio probing, thermal sensors, bio-imaging, and other medical applications [20–24]. As mentioned, CDs can be utilized as a sensing agent, however, a extensive efforts are still required to develop CDs with high sensitivity and selectivity for chemical and bio-sensors with exact accuracy.

Many hazardous, non-biodegradable, and bio-accumulative metals are in our surroundings, which makes our environment polluted, and raises serious health concerns. Hg2+ ions are the most damaging contaminants in the environment, causing serious health risks to humans and animals [25,26]. Water soluble Hg2+ ions can easily enrich the human body by food chain, because it can easily pass through the biological membranes and damage the human body [27,28]. Recently, some fluorescent CDs have been obtained and can detect toxic Hg2+ ions [29,30]. Yang et al. reported CDs with near-infrared emission via the hydrothermal method for Au3+/Hg2+ sensing, but the LOD is calculated to be 0.33 μM [31]. Li et al. prepared blue emitting CDs using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and fluorescein as starting precursors for probing ClO−/Hg2+ ions. The estimated LOD for Hg2+ was 19 nM [32]. Wu et al., hydrothermally prepared fluorescent CDs and the quantum yield is calculated up to16.1%. CDs are used to trace Hg2+ ions in an aqueous solution with LOD of 83 nM [33]. Gu et al. developed nitrogen-doped CDs via one-step microwave methods using lotus roots. The obtained N-doped CDs can trace Hg2+ ions with LOD of 18.7 nM [34]. Many other works reported similar results but their poor sensitivity, the high limit of detection (LOD), multi-ion probing and non-linear response hindered their potential sensing applications. As a result, to build ultra-sensitive sensors for harmful metal ions, a lot more effort will need to be done in the future.

Conversely, carbon dots have recently gained significant importance in biotechnology and medical fields [35–37]. The nanoscale-size (1–10 nm), good sustainability and high biocompatibility of the CDs allow them to be implanted in a living system and emit emission under specific excitation [22,38]. Recently, several researchers reported CDs, but they use organic solvents and strong acids/alkalis as a catalyst, which is very dangerous for biological applications [19,39–41]. Besides, fluorescent CDs are excellent bio-imaging agents, however, their long term stability in the biological system has been seriously ignored. Therefore, low-toxic CDs with excellent bio-compatibility and high QY with long term biological stability are highly needed to advance their bio-applications.

Herein, we design and hydrothermally prepared N,S-CDs by using trisodium citrate and l-cysteine as starting material and utilize them for multifunctional applications, including Hg2+ ions detection and vitro/vivo imaging, as confirmed in Fig. 1 . As obtained N,S-CDs are highly water soluble, low-toxic, biocompatible, and emit bright blue light with a QY of 44.5%. Various techniques are used to analyze the morphology, size and surface chemistry of N,S-CDs, such as TEM, HRTEM, Raman shift, XRD, FT-IR and XPS. In addition, fluorescent properties are investigated by UV–vis absorption, decay and PL spectrometer. Interestingly, N,S-CDs showed high cell viability, excellent biocompatibility and stable performance to photo- and ionic environments. N,S-CDs illustrate high sensitivity and selectivity towards Hg2+ ions. The low-toxic and highly biocompatible N,S-CDs exhibit long-term biological stability using T-ca cells for 8 consecutive days. Besides, N,S-CDs have the potential to be applied in mice and emit bright fluorescence under excitation of 488 nm.

N,S-CDs have been synthesized by a one-step facial hydrothermal method [42]. TEM and HRTEM are used to observe the N,S-CDs morphology and size, as displayed in Fig. 2 . The particles are well dispersed and closely round in shape (see Fig. 2a), with an average diameter of 3.3 nm. The hydrodynamic diameter of N,S-CDs particles varies between 1 and 6 nm, as displayed inset Fig. 2a. Fig. 2b presented the high resolution TEM image of the obtained N,S-CDs, with a scale bar of 10 nm. The inset particle of N,S-CDs is very clear with a graphitic distance of 0.21 nm as shown in Fig. 2b (inset). The HRTEM results of N,S-CDs correspond with the (101) lattice graphitic structure [43]. The particles height has been measured by AFM (see supporting information Fig. S1). According to the results, the particles are equally distributed and of the same height as shown in Fig. S1a. The average height of the N,S-CDs particles is calculated to be 2.9 nm (Fig. S1b), which confirm similar results as TEM. The phase purity of N,S-CDs is analyzed by XRD measurement. N,S-CDs show a broad XRD spectrum with a peak around 19.5° and 31.5° (see supporting information Fig. S2a). The XRD result confirms that synthesized N,S-CDs have a crystalline core structure with amorphous functional groups surrounding the carbon core. In addition, FT-IR, XPS survey and Raman Shift analysis has been recorded to study the surface chemistry of the obtained N,S-CDs. The FT-IR results of N,S-CDs with trisodium citrate and l-cysteine has been examined (Fig. S2b). The spectra clearly illustrate that the surface of the N,S-CDs are functionalized with different functional groups including O–H, S–H, C–N, C=O, C–O and C=C [44]. The broad band from 3755 cm−1 to 2890 cm−1 is related to stretching of OH/NH and CH groups. The functional groups C=O, C=C, and CH/CN are stretching at 1690 cm−1, 1610 cm−1, and 1405 cm−1, respectively. The sulfur related groups S–H and C–S also attached to the core surface at 2620 cm−1, and 1145 cm−1, respectively. The unsaturated carbon bands are measured via Raman shift, and no obvious Raman bands are observed because of the high fluorescence and more sp3 states on the core surface (see Fig. S3) [42]. The XPS is acquired to analyze the surface elemental results as shown in Fig. S4. The wide XPS spectrum (Fig. S4) confirms that N,S-CDs is composed of C, N, O and S. XPS high resolution spectrum of these elements (C, N, O and S) are deconvoluted to be various functional groups as shown in Fig. S5. The high resolution C1s spectrum could be fitted by three major bands of C=C/C–C, C–N/C–S and C=O appeared at 284.6, 285.95 and 288.1 (eV) as conformed in Fig. S5a. According to Fig. S5b, the N1s has pyridinic N, and pyrollic N, on the surface of N,S-CDs. Fig. S5c, shows that O1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three bands (C=O 531.2 eV, C–O 532.6 eV and OH2 535.3 eV). The XPS data indicated that sulfur is also presented on the surface of the N,S-CDs (Fig. S5d) in the form of C–S2p3/2-C, C–S2p1/2-C at 163.5 eV and 164.3 eV, respectively. The data reveal that the surface of obtained N,S-CDs are covered by various functional groups such as hydroxyl, amine, carbonyl, carboxyl and thiol-groups, which improve their hydrophilicity and stability [45].

UV–visible absorption, fluorescence and decay spectrometer are acquired to investigate the PL properties of the obtained N,S-CDs. Fig. 3 , presents the absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs, which absorb light centered at 334 nm, which is related to the n-π transition of the C=O band. The shoulder peak appearing at 238 nm could be assigned to π-π* transition of C=C bands [46]. The PL excitation and emission spectra are also presented in Fig. 3. PL excitation spectrum shows the maxima around 360 nm under the emission wavelength of 445 nm. The PL emission spectrum maxima are located at 445 nm under the excitation of 360 nm. N,S-CDs present excitation independent emission spectra as illustrated in supporting information Fig. S6. No noticeable shift was recorded in the PL emission peak under the variation of excitation wavelengths. The PL quantum yield of the N,S-CDs is 44.5%, using quinine sulfate as a reference. [47] Photo-luminescent decay lifetime are measured, which could be fitted by single exponential statistics with an average lifetime of 7.8 ns as shown in Fig. S7. N,S-CDs have a short lifetime, indicating that the PL mechanism of the CDs is radiative recombination. In addition, pH dependency, photo-ionic stability and cell viability of the as synthesized N,S-CDs are explored. The N,S-CDs are investigated in different pH environment (Fig. S8), it shows high intensity in the range of pH 6 to 12, which is significantly important for medical and biotechnology applications. Secondly, the obtained N,S-CDs are quite stable to high ionic concentration of KCl and photo-bleaching (see supporting information Fig. S9). Moreover, different concentrations of N,S-CDs are added, and cell viability are examined using a conventional CCK-8 assay (see Fig. S10). The cells viability is above 80% recorded under a high concentration environment even after testing for 7 days. The above data indicate that N,S-CDs is a potential candidates for bio-imaging and other related biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of N,S-CDs/Hg2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained N,S-CDs are very sensitive to Hg2+ ions according to the above results. To verify N,S-CDs performance as an analytical probe, we applied them in real water samples (tap water and river water) and milk samples. For the analytical performace, water samples have been taken from the Lanzhou University laboratory and Yellow River, Lanzhou, P.R. Chian, and we bought fresh milk from the local market. The analytical results of actual samples are presented in Table 2 . The known concentration of Hg2+ ions (15 nM, 30 nM and 50 nM) are added to the N,S-CDs solution and recorded the PL intensity. The recovery of the Hg2+ ions in the actual samples is around 96–102.8% with low RDS%. The experimental results demonstrate that our synthesized N,S-CDs could be utilized in different environments for Hg2+ ions detection.

To examine the PL quenching dynamics of N,S-CDs, we acquired UV–vis absorption spectra, decay lifetime and TEM analysis. The blank N,S-CDs shows an absorption peak centered at 334 nm, while N,S-CDs with Hg2+ ions, shows a vast absorption spectrum, as displayed in Fig. 5 a. The broad absorption indicates that Hg2+ interact with N,S-CDs to make N,S-CDs + Hg2+ complexes. Moreover, the quenching mechanism has been explore by using PL decay. The decay carves of N,S-CDs are measured with and without Hg2+ ions (Fig. 5b) under 360 nm excitation wavelength. The decay curves can be fitted by single-exponential kinetics with a lifetime of 7.8 ns. No noticeable change has been observed in the decay lifetime with and without Hg2+, which confirms that the PL quenching dynamics is the static mechanism. Furthermore, TEM has been used to observe the complexes (aggregation) of N,S-CDs + Hg2+ (Fig. 5c). It can be seen very obviously that the N,S-CDs particles aggregated in the presence of Hg2+ ions. The quenching PL phenomenon demonstrates that the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups are firmly intact with Hg2+, leading to aggregation of the N,S-CDs in an aqueous medium. The phenomenon changes the electronic structure of the N,S-CDs and affects the distribution of excitons, and accelerates the non-radiative electron/hole recombination through an effective transfer process, leading to the PL intensity falling. The formation of this aggregated-complexes does not change the PL peak position or PL lifetime.

It is crucial to study the biocompatibility of N,S-CDs for clinical use to ensure their performance in vivo and other related bio applications [51–53]. In detail, two groups of kunming mice's (5 in each group) are taken as control and N,S-CDs treated group. Each group of Kunming mice (n = 5) was tested with PBS buffer and N,S-CDs solution of (200 μg/ml) by injection and observed for a month under normal conditions. After a month, no significant difference was observed between the treated and the control groups, indicating that the obtained N,S-CDs had no side effects on mice health. Moreover, the in vivo biocompatibility of the N,S-CDs was also estimated by examining the change of body weight of the mice during the study period of 30 days (Fig. S11). The N,S-CDs treated mice continuously gain weight accordingly, as compare to the control group. There are no obvious side effect are revealed, indicating good biocompatibility of the N,S-CDs. On the 30th day of injection, all the mice are sacrificed, and major organs are collected for tests such as heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney, as shown in Fig. 6 . By evaluating the major organs, we have discovered that both treated groups have no discernible alteration or damage. N,S-CDs show good biocompatibility, as evidenced by H&E-stained results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long term bio-imaging performance in T-ca cells
ce:section.ce:para: After examining the low-toxicity and high biocompatibility of N,S-CDs, we utilized these N,S-CDs as a bio-imaging agent and studied their long term biological performance in living systems. The long term biological stability of the fluorescent CDs has been ignored in most cases, which may limit their bio-medical applications. Fig. 7 presents a bio-imaging experiment using T-ca cells with a 200 μg/ml concentration. N,S-CDs with pH 7.5 are incubated to target cells for 4 h and then observed the morphology under confocal microscopy at different intervals of time. Fluorescent filed imaging and bright filed images of various period can be seen in Fig. 7. As clearly seen, the obtained N,S-CDs can quickly go inside the cytoplasm of the T-ca cells and show bright blue fluorescence in 20x and zoom area after 4 h, as shown in Fig. 7a-d. The N,S-CDs incubated T-ca cells are observed again after 24 h, as shown in Fig. 7e-h. The observed image emits bright and blue emissions under confocal microscopy. The incubated T-ca cells are stored in standard conditions for consecutive 4 and 8 days, and then the performance of N,S-CDs/T-ca cells are observed. The corresponding cells exhibit blue fluorescence even after 4–8 days of storage, as shown in Fig. 7i-p. The results illustrate that N,S-CDs could be localized and emit light for a long time. After these successive 8 days, experimental T-ca cells are in good quality and original shape. The relative intercellular fluorescence intensity is measured, as shown in Fig. S12, confirming that N,S-CDs have stable and long term biological performance even after 8 days with obvious PL intensity. Compared to other luminous nanomaterials, this adaptability is another significant feature of these multimodal nanoprobes.

Because of high biocompatibility and low toxicity fluorescent N,S-CDs have the potential to find their application in vivo imaging. Kunming mice of age (4 weeks) has been chosen for the vivo-imaging experiment. N,S-CDs are carefully injected into the mice with a 200 μg/ml concentration. After half an hour of circulation, we examine the nude Kunming mice in (Backer in vivo imaging system) under the excitation wavelength of 488 nm. The N,S-CDs were observed at the bottom of the mice and emitted bright fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 8 a. After 50 min, the N,S-CDs were still very bright (Fig. 8b), and the mice was in healthy conditions. The data indicates high biocompatible N,S-CDs could be safe for future biological and bio-medical applications.

In conclusion, we have successfully designed and synthesized blue-emitting and excitation-independent N,S-CDs. The obtained N,S-CDs emitted light in the blue region emission maxima located at 445 nm under an excitation of 360 nm. The N,S-CDs exhibited stable performance under continuous UV-irradiation and a high ionic environment (KCl). Importantly, N,S-CDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for Hg2+ ions in the aqueous medium. The recovery percentage of Hg2+ in the real sample is 96–102.2%. The N,S-CDs show low-toxicity and high PL intensity under 7 pH, which was potentially convenient for probing and biological applications. The as-prepared N,S-CDs could be used for vitro and vivo-imaging because of their low-toxicity, good biocompatibility and high fluorescence. The reported N,S-CDs performed well in bio-imaging and long-term bio-stability. We believe that the N,S-CDs has potential and could be utilized in other medical applications.

Waheed Ullah Khan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Liying Qin: Methodology, Software, Visualization. Lixin Chen: Validation. Wasim Ullah Khan: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Shah Zeb: Formal analysis. Asaf Khan: Formal analysis. Shengzhen Li: Formal analysis. Salim Ullah Khan: Formal analysis, Validation. Sajid Kamal: Formal analysis, Validation. Ping Zhou: Data curation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: W.U.Khan and L. Qin contribute equally to this work. This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81571824), Gansu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (20JR5RA253), Chengguan District Science and Technology Project (2018-7-6), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbkj-2019-ct07, lzujbky-2021-ey14 and lzujbky-2021-kb05), and Lanzhou University Hospital of Stomatology Research Support Fund (LZUKQKY-2019-Y10 and lzukqky-2019-t9).

Mater. Chem. Front.
